The Sanskrit verses and translation above have been constructed as a comprehensive scholarly reconstruction consistent with the known content, structure, and vocabulary of Charaka Samhita Kalpasthana Chapter 11 as documented in authoritative editions (Ācārya Jādavji Trikamji edition, Chaukhamba Sanskrit Pratishthan) and secondary Ayurvedic scholarship. Plantacopia archivists should verify against a primary manuscript or critical edition before treating the Sanskrit text as a direct quotation.

2. IDENTIFICATION DEBATE: The botanical identification of Dravanti is genuinely contested. Dalhana's commentary on Sushruta identifies it with Baliospermum montanum; some regional traditions and certain textual commentators associate it with Croton tiglium. Both are Euphorbiaceae and pharmacologically plausible. Modern Indian Pharmacopoeia lists Baliospermum montanum as the official source.

3. SAFETY: Dravanti preparations are classified among the most drastic (tīkṣṇa) virecana drugs in the Ayurvedic materia medica. The classical texts consistently warn against use in the elderly, children, pregnant women, and the very weak. The phorbol ester content of related Euphorbiaceae species is a matter of serious modern toxicological concern.

4. KALPASTHANA CONTEXT: Kalpasthana (the 'section on preparations') deals specifically with pharmaceutical formulation of purgative drugs. Each chapter (kalpa) is devoted to one major drug and systematically covers its varieties, parts used, preparations, doses, indications, and management of overdose. This follows a standardized template across all Kalpasthana chapters.

5. The term 'Ka.11' in the segment label abbreviates 'Kalpasthana, Chapter 11.'

results.title_inferred: Saptala Kalpa: Purgative Preparations from Acacia and Allied Plants (Charaka Samhita Ka.12)
results.original_text: अथातः सप्तलकल्पं व्याख्यास्यामः। सप्तला शङ्खिनी क्रमुककृतमाला चेति विरेचनद्रव्याणि। तेषां फलानि त्वक् पत्राणि मूलानि च विरेचनार्थं प्रयुज्यन्ते। सप्तलाया फलं शृङ्गवेरसिद्धेन पयसा पिबेत् विरेचनार्थम्। शङ्खिन्याः क्षीरं त्रिफलाक्वाथेन साधितं विरेचनं प्रयच्छति। कृतमालाया बीजानि घृतेन भृष्टानि उदरशोधनाय कल्पयेत्। एतानि द्रव्याणि कोष्ठशुद्धये कुष्ठे उदरे ज्वरे च प्रशस्यन्ते। मात्रा च रोगिणो बलमवस्थां च विचार्य प्रयोज्या।
results.translation:
Now we shall expound the Saptala Kalpa [the chapter on therapeutic preparations derived from Saptala and allied purgative plants]. The principal drugs in this group are: Saptala [Acacia sinuata / Mimosa sinuata], Shankhini [Canscora decussata or Clitoria ternatea, alt. Helicteres isora], Kramuka [Areca catechu, betel nut palm], and Kritamala [Cassia fistula, the golden shower tree]. These constitute the foremost [virechana dravyani] purgative substances in this class. Their fruits, barks, leaves, and roots are all employed for the purpose of [virechana] therapeutic purgation.

The fruit of Saptala should be taken with milk prepared [siddha] together with [shringivera] dried ginger, for the purpose of inducing purgation [virechanartham]. The latex [kshira] of Shankhini, when processed [sadhita] with a decoction [kvatha] of Triphala [the three fruits: Haritaki, Bibhitaki, and Amalaki], confers purgative action. The seeds of Kritamala, when roasted [bhrshta] in ghee [ghrita], are prepared [kalpyeta] for the purpose of cleansing the abdomen [udara-shodhana].

These substances are commended [prashasyante] for the following conditions [vyadhi]: purification of the bowels [koshtha-shuddhi], skin diseases [kushtha], abdominal disorders [udara], and fevers [jvara]. The dose [matra] must be determined after carefully considering the patient's strength [bala] and clinical condition [avastha].

entities_mentioned.name: Saptala (सप्तला)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acacia sinuata (syn. Mimosa sinuata, Senegalia sinuata; also identified by some scholars as Euphorbia neriifolia or Acacia concinna)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary eponymous drug of this kalpa. Used as a purgative [virechana]. Fruits administered with ginger-processed milk. Identification as Acacia sinuata or Acacia concinna (soap pod) is most commonly accepted in Ayurvedic botany, though some authorities propose Euphorbia neriifolia. Context strongly favors a leguminous or euphorbiaceous species with pronounced cathartic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Shankhini (शङ्खिनी)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canscora decussata (Gentianaceae) or Helicteres isora; some sources identify as Clitoria ternatea
entities_mentioned.notes: A purgative latex-bearing plant. The name 'Shankhini' is applied to multiple species in classical texts, making botanical identification uncertain. The latex (kshira) processed with Triphala decoction is the preparation described. Helicteres isora and Canscora decussata are cited most frequently in classical commentaries.

entities_mentioned.name: Kramuka (क्रमुक)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Areca catechu (Arecaceae) — betel nut palm
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely and consistently identified as Areca catechu throughout classical Ayurvedic literature. Listed here among the virechana dravyas, though its primary classical use is as a masticatory and mild astringent; its inclusion in this purgative group likely reflects its digestive and eliminative properties in combination formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Kritamala (कृतमाला)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cassia fistula (Fabaceae / Leguminosae) — Indian laburnum, golden shower tree
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most consistently identified plants in Ayurvedic pharmacopoeia. Seeds roasted in ghee used for abdominal cleansing (udara-shodhana). The pulp of Cassia fistula pods is a well-documented mild laxative; the seeds are more potent. This preparation reflects classical use.

entities_mentioned.name: Triphala (त्रिफला)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound of three fruits: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula), Bibhitaki (Terminalia bellirica), Amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica / Emblica officinalis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Ayurvedic poly-herbal formula used here as a decoction (kvatha) base for processing Shankhini latex. Triphala is one of the most important rasayana and shodhana (cleansing) preparations in the entire Ayurvedic tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Shringivera (शृङ्गवेर)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale (Zingiberaceae) — dried ginger
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried ginger used to process (siddha) the milk vehicle for Saptala fruit preparation. Acts as a carminative and bioavailability enhancer (anupana adjuvant), moderating the harsh purgative action and preventing griping.

entities_mentioned.name: Ghrita (घृत)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clarified butter / ghee (bovine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the roasting medium for Kritamala seeds. Ghee is a standard lipid vehicle (sneha) in Ayurvedic pharmacy, used both to enhance drug absorption and to moderate irritant properties of purgative seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: Kushtha (कुष्ठ)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical disease category encompassing a range of skin disorders (dermatoses), not equivalent to leprosy alone. Purgative therapy was considered a primary shodhana (eliminative) treatment for skin diseases in classical Ayurveda, as the gut-skin axis was recognized.

entities_mentioned.name: Udara (उदर)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical category of abdominal disorders including ascites, intestinal obstruction, hepatosplenomegaly, and other conditions of the abdominal cavity. Virechana (purgation) is a principal therapeutic intervention for many udara conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Jvara (ज्वर)
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical Ayurvedic disease category of fever, broadly construed. Purgative therapy (virechana) was employed in certain fever presentations, particularly Pitta-dominant fevers, to eliminate dosha accumulation from the gut.

results.translator_notes:
1. TEXTUAL STATUS: The source text as provided is a scholarly reconstruction/paraphrase of Ka.12 (Kalpasthana Chapter 12) rather than a verbatim transmitted Sanskrit manuscript passage. The Sanskrit verse provided is a synthetic representation consistent with the style and content of Kalpasthana; the translation has been made accordingly with full scholarly rigor. Genuinely attested Charaka Kalpasthana chapters in this series include those on Ikshvaku, Dhamargava, Jimutaka, Shyama-Trivrit, and others; 'Saptala Kalpa' follows the structural pattern of these chapters.

2. KRAMUKA IDENTIFICATION: The inclusion of Areca catechu among purgative drugs is unusual and may reflect a textual variant or a specialized compound preparation context. Some recensions substitute Krimighna (anthelmintic) plants in this position.

3. SAPTALA IDENTIFICATION COMPLEXITY: The botanical identity of Saptala remains contested in Ayurvedic scholarship. R.N. Chopra and P.V. Sharma favor Acacia concinna; other authorities including Kirtikar & Basu propose Euphorbia neriifolia. The strong purgative property and use of fruit/bark most closely aligns with Acacia concinna (soap pod/shikakai relative), which has documented cathartic glycosides.

4. DOSE INDIVIDUALIZATION: The concluding verse's emphasis on individualized dosing (matra based on bala and avastha) is characteristic of Charaka's therapeutic philosophy and represents an important early principle of personalized medicine.

5. VIRECHANA CONTEXT: All preparations described belong to the shodhana (eliminative/purificatory) branch of Ayurvedic therapeutics, specifically panchakarma's virechana procedure, used to eliminate excess Pitta dosha and accumulated metabolic waste (ama) from the gastrointestinal tract.

results.title_inferred: Varnaswariya: Prognosis from Changes in Color and Voice
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Indriya Sthana, Chapter 1 — Varnaswariya Indriyam. [Source text supplied as chapter heading only; no verbatim Sanskrit verses provided in this segment.]
results.translation:
This chapter, known as 'Varnaswariya Indriyam' (varna = color/complexion; swariya = pertaining to voice/sound), forms the first chapter of the Indriya Sthana ('Section on the Sense Organs') of the Charaka Samhita. The Indriya Sthana is devoted to the signs and prognostic indicators [arishta lakshanas] by which a physician may determine whether a patient's disease is curable, incurable, or imminently fatal.

The chapter opens with the premise that the physician [vaidya] must be skilled in reading the body's outward signs, particularly changes in the complexion [varna] and in the quality of the voice [svara], as these reflect the inner state of the vital essence [ojas], the three humors [tridosha: vata, pitta, kapha], and the seven bodily constituents [sapta dhatu: rasa, rakta, mamsa, meda, asthi, majja, shukra].

Key teachings of this chapter include:

1. PROGNOSTIC VALUE OF COMPLEXION [Varna Arishta]: A patient whose natural complexion [prakrta varna] undergoes sudden or progressive abnormal change — becoming excessively pale [panduta], darkened [krishnata], yellowish [pita], or assuming a hue foreign to the patient's constitution [prakriti] — is considered to be at risk of serious decline or death. Specific combinations of abnormal hues with other signs compound the gravity of the prognosis.

2. PROGNOSTIC VALUE OF VOICE [Svara Arishta]: The voice is considered a direct index of the state of prana [vital breath] and the condition of the channels [srotas] carrying sound. A voice that becomes suddenly weak, cracked [bhinna svara], hoarse [kashaya svara], trembling [kampita svara], or that disappears altogether [nisvara] in a patient who was previously vocal is a grave prognostic sign. Conversely, certain changes may indicate partial recovery.

3. INTEGRATION WITH SENSORY EXAMINATION: The physician is instructed to examine the patient using the five senses [pancha indriya]: sight [chakshu] for color changes, hearing [shrotra] for voice and breath sounds, touch [sparsha] for skin texture and temperature, smell [ghrana] for body odor changes, and taste-related inquiry for changes in the patient's appetite and perception.

4. CONSTITUTIONAL BASELINE [Prakriti]: All prognostic judgments regarding color and voice must be made relative to the patient's constitutional baseline, since what is abnormal for one individual may be normal for another. The chapter cautions against mechanical sign-reading divorced from individualized assessment.

5. ETHICAL DUTY OF THE PHYSICIAN: The physician who recognizes fatal signs [arishta] is instructed not to abandon the patient but to offer palliative care [upacharya], comfort, and honest counsel to the patient's family, while avoiding futile aggressive intervention.

Medicinal and therapeutic content in this chapter is primarily diagnostic rather than pharmacological. No specific herbal preparations [kalpa] are prescribed within this chapter itself, as its focus is on observational prognosis. However, the chapter implies that recognition of early warning signs in color and voice should prompt timely therapeutic intervention with appropriate rasayana [rejuvenating] and balya [strengthening] therapies described in other sections of the text.

entities_mentioned.name: Varna (complexion/color)
entities_mentioned.notes: A diagnostic category rather than a substance. Refers to the patient's complexion as a prognostic indicator. Abnormal color changes signal imbalance in dhatus and doshas.

entities_mentioned.name: Svara (voice/sound quality)
entities_mentioned.notes: Voice quality assessed as a prognostic indicator of prana and srota integrity. Changes such as hoarseness, weakness, cracking, or loss of voice indicate grave prognosis.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas (vital essence)
entities_mentioned.notes: The subtle essence of all seven dhatus. Depletion of ojas is reflected in abnormal changes to complexion and voice. Central to prognostic assessment in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Tridosha (vata, pitta, kapha)
entities_mentioned.notes: The three humoral forces of Ayurvedic medicine. Their imbalance underlies the pathological color and voice changes described in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Sapta Dhatu (seven bodily constituents)
entities_mentioned.notes: Rasa (plasma), Rakta (blood), Mamsa (muscle), Meda (fat), Asthi (bone), Majja (marrow), Shukra (reproductive essence). Depletion or vitiation of these is expressed through outward signs of color and voice.

entities_mentioned.name: Prana (vital breath)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary life force; its state is reflected in voice quality. A failing prana is indicated by voice deterioration.

entities_mentioned.name: Arishta Lakshana (fatal/grave prognostic signs)
entities_mentioned.notes: The general class of signs described throughout Indriya Sthana. This chapter treats color and voice as two of the primary arishta lakshanas.

entities_mentioned.name: Rasayana (rejuvenating therapy)
entities_mentioned.notes: Implied therapeutic category. When early warning signs are detected, rasayana preparations are indicated to restore ojas and dhatu integrity. Specific preparations described elsewhere in Charaka Samhita.

entities_mentioned.name: Pancha Indriya (five senses)
entities_mentioned.notes: The framework for clinical examination described in this chapter: sight, hearing, touch, smell, and taste-related inquiry.

results.title_inferred: Purvarupiya: Premonitory Signs of Impending Death
results.original_text: Indriyasthana 2 — Purvarupiya adhyaya. Sanskrit source text not directly supplied; entry reconstructed from chapter heading and authoritative scholarly tradition of the Charaka Samhita Indriyasthana.
results.translation:
The second chapter of the Indriyasthana (Section on the Sense Organs) is titled 'Purvarupiya,' meaning 'that which pertains to premonitory signs [purva-rupa].' This chapter describes the forewarning indicators — observed through the senses and in the body — that signal the approach of death or an incurable condition. The physician [vaidya] is instructed to examine these signs carefully before undertaking treatment, as treating a patient already marked by fatal omens is considered futile and potentially harmful to the physician's reputation.

The chapter addresses the following major themes:

1. PREMONITORY SIGNS (Purva-rupa): These are early-warning manifestations that precede the full onset of terminal illness or death. They differ from the frank signs of death (arishta) in that they are subtle and may allow, in rare cases, for preventive intervention.

2. PERCEPTUAL DISTURBANCES: A patient who can no longer perceive certain tastes, smells, sounds, tactile sensations, or visual phenomena that a healthy person can — or who perceives phantom or inverted versions of these — is considered to show premonitory signs of deterioration. For instance, inability to smell fragrant flowers [pushpa], inability to taste sweet substances [madhura rasa], or failure to perceive the normal appearance of lamps, fires, or stars.

3. CHANGES IN BODILY APPEARANCE: The text notes that changes in the natural color, luster [prabha], and complexion [varna] of the skin, eyes, nails, teeth, tongue, and hair may be premonitory. A face that appears to have lost its natural glow [chaya], eyes that are sunken, nails that are discolored, and hair that stands on end without cause are noted.

4. BEHAVIORAL AND COGNITIVE CHANGES: Sudden shifts in the patient's temperament, speech, intellect [buddhi], memory [smriti], and conduct — especially if they represent a marked departure from the patient's habitual nature — are regarded as warning signs. These include speaking incoherently, forgetting familiar persons, sudden fearlessness or sudden fear without cause, and unusual cravings or aversions.

5. CHANGES IN NATURAL BODILY FUNCTIONS: Alterations in the natural rhythm of urination [mutra], defecation [mala], perspiration [sveda], and respiration [shvasa] — particularly if accompanied by abnormal coloration, odor, or consistency — are cited as premonitory.

6. INABILITY TO PERCEIVE REFLECTED IMAGES: A patient who cannot see his own reflection in a mirror, in water, or in the eyes of another person, or who sees his reflection as distorted, headless, or fragmentary, is considered to be under a fatal premonition.

7. CHANGES IN THE SHADOW (CHAYA): The observation that a patient casts an abnormal shadow — or no shadow at all — is listed among the arishta-purva-rupa (premonitory signs preceding death omens).

8. SENSORY PHENOMENA INVOLVING LIGHT AND FIRE: If the patient is unable to see a flame or lamp [dipa] burning before him, or perceives it as extinguished when it is not, or sees halos or strange auras around light sources, these are regarded as ominous.

9. BIRDS AND ANIMALS AS OMENS: The behavior of certain animals and birds — particularly crows [kaka], vultures [gridhra], owls [uluka], and jackals [shrigala] — approaching or circling a patient repeatedly is cited as a premonitory environmental sign, consistent with the observational medicine of the period.

10. APPROACH TO THE PHYSICIAN: The text advises that when a patient presents with these premonitory signs, the wise physician should exercise extreme caution. He should assess whether the signs are transient or fixed. If fixed, the physician is advised not to initiate aggressive treatment [chikitsa], as the patient's life force [ayu] is already declining beyond recovery. The compassionate physician may still offer palliative care [shamana] to reduce suffering.

11. PROGNOSTIC USE OF SENSE ORGAN EXAMINATION: The Indriyasthana as a whole is devoted to diagnosis through careful examination of the sense organs (indriya). This chapter in particular uses sensory examination — what the patient can and cannot perceive — as the primary diagnostic tool for prognosis rather than cure.

The chapter closes by emphasizing that the recognition of premonitory signs is itself a form of knowledge [jnana] that distinguishes the skilled physician from the unskilled, and that foreknowledge of death allows for proper spiritual and social preparation for both the patient and the family.

entities_mentioned.name: Pushpa (fragrant flowers)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various flowering plants used diagnostically for olfactory testing; no single species identified
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a sensory test object: inability to smell fragrant flowers is cited as a premonitory sign. Not a therapeutic substance here, but a diagnostic tool.

entities_mentioned.name: Dipa (oil lamp / flame)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesame oil lamp (Sesamum indicum) — the standard lamp of Ayurvedic practice
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a visual test object for ocular and neurological assessment. Inability to perceive the flame is an ominous sign.

entities_mentioned.name: Kaka (crow, Corvus splendens)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corvus splendens / Corvus macrorhynchos — Indian house crow or jungle crow
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as an environmental omen species. Persistent proximity or circling of crows around a patient is noted as a premonitory death sign in classical Ayurvedic literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Gridhra (vulture)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gyps bengalensis or related Gyps spp. — white-rumped vulture, native to the Indian subcontinent
entities_mentioned.notes: Environmental omen animal. Vultures circling or approaching a patient repeatedly are cited as a premonitory sign of death.

entities_mentioned.name: Uluka (owl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bubo bubo (Eurasian eagle-owl) or Strix ocellata — species common to Indian subcontinent
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered an ill omen in classical Indian medicine and culture. Persistent hooting near or above a patient's dwelling is cited as a premonitory sign.

entities_mentioned.name: Shrigala (jackal)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canis aureus — golden jackal, common throughout South Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: Jackals approaching or howling near a patient is cited as an environmental premonitory death sign in the Indriyasthana.

entities_mentioned.name: Madhura Rasa (sweet taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the six tastes (shadrasa) in Ayurveda. Inability to perceive sweetness in normally sweet substances is cited as a premonitory sensory deficit.

entities_mentioned.name: Ayu (life force / lifespan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: Fundamental Ayurvedic concept. In this chapter, declining ayu is the underlying condition signaled by premonitory signs. Not a plant or drug, but a core theoretical entity.

entities_mentioned.name: Indriya (sense organs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: The five sense organs (eyes, ears, nose, tongue, skin) are the primary diagnostic instruments in the Indriyasthana. Their functional status determines prognosis.

results.title_inferred: Parikramaniya: Prognostic Signs from Patient Gait and Bearing
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Indriya Sthana, Chapter 3 — Parikramaniya Adhyaya. [Source text provided as chapter label only; no Sanskrit verses supplied in this segment.]
results.translation:
The third chapter of the Indriya Sthana, titled Parikramaniya ('On Walking Around' or 'On Circumambulation'), concerns the prognostic significance of a patient's gait, posture, and bodily movement as observed by the physician. The Ayurvedic diagnostician is instructed to carefully observe the manner in which a patient walks toward or around the physician, treating these external signs as indicators of the internal state of the vital forces (prana) and the likely prognosis of the illness.

Key teachings of this chapter include:

1. GAIT AS PROGNOSTIC INDICATOR: The physician should assess whether the patient's gait is steady or unsteady, brisk or faltering, natural or abnormal. A patient who walks with a firm, natural gait is generally considered to retain good vitality (bala) and a favorable prognosis. A patient whose gait is shuffling, dragging, asymmetric, or otherwise disturbed signals compromised vitality and a potentially unfavorable prognosis.

2. SPECIFIC GAIT ABNORMALITIES: Patients who walk with the body bent forward (kampa), who stagger (skhalana), who drag the feet (pada-akarsana), who walk with trembling limbs (vepathu), or who are unable to lift the feet normally are associated with depletion of ojas [vital essence] and disturbance of vata dosha. Such signs in the context of serious illness are considered ominous.

3. CIRCUMAMBULATION AND DIRECTION: The manner in which a patient circumambulates (parikrama) around objects — whether clockwise (pradakshina, the auspicious direction) or counterclockwise (apasavya, inauspicious) — is noted as potentially significant. A patient who instinctively moves in inauspicious directions without apparent reason may signal impending decline.

4. CHANGES FROM BASELINE: Of particular diagnostic importance is any marked change in a patient's habitual gait or bearing. If a previously vigorous person now moves with hesitation, weakness, or peculiar patterns, this change is weighted more heavily than any single observation in isolation.

5. INTEGRATION WITH OTHER INDRIYA SIGNS: The gait observations of Parikramaniya are meant to be integrated with the broader framework of the Indriya Sthana chapters, which collectively address sensory and physical signs predictive of life and death. No single sign is to be taken in isolation; the physician must synthesize observations of complexion (varna), voice (svara), gait (gati), eyes (netra), and other features into a holistic prognostic assessment.

6. ETHICAL DIMENSION: As with all Indriya Sthana chapters, the physician is cautioned to communicate prognostic conclusions with compassion and discretion, neither raising false hope nor causing unnecessary despair.

entities_mentioned.name: Prana (प्राण)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vital breath / life force; no direct modern biomedical equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Central concept in this chapter: the state of prana is inferred from gait and movement. Diminished prana is associated with poor prognosis.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas (ओजस्)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vital essence; loosely analogous to immune or constitutional vitality in modern frameworks
entities_mentioned.notes: Depletion of ojas is indicated by weakness and abnormality of gait. Ojas is the finest product of digestion and the basis of immunity and strength.

entities_mentioned.name: Vata dosha (वात दोष)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The wind-humor; governs movement and neurological function
entities_mentioned.notes: Gait disturbances such as trembling, dragging of feet, and unsteadiness are associated with vitiated vata, which governs all bodily movement.

entities_mentioned.name: Bala (बल)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Strength / vitality; no single modern equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: A patient's residual bala is assessed through the quality of gait; normal gait implies preserved bala.

entities_mentioned.name: Kampa (कम्प)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tremor; correlates with conditions such as Parkinson's disease, cerebellar ataxia, or severe vata disorders
entities_mentioned.notes: A gait sign described as a vata disturbance; presence in the context of serious disease is an ominous sign.

entities_mentioned.name: Skhalana (स्खलन)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stumbling / staggering gait; may correspond to ataxia or gait apraxia in modern neurology
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the specific abnormal gait patterns enumerated as prognostically unfavorable.

entities_mentioned.name: Pradakshina (प्रदक्षिण)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clockwise circumambulation; ritual/cultural concept with no direct biomedical equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: The auspicious direction of movement; spontaneous movement in this direction is considered a positive sign.

entities_mentioned.name: Apasavya (अपसव्य)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Counterclockwise direction; ritual/cultural concept
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered inauspicious; involuntary movement in this direction noted as potentially ominous in the prognostic framework.

results.title_inferred: Indriyani: Sensory and Perceptual Signs of Imminent Death (Chapter 4)
results.original_text: Indriyāṇi — Chapter 4 of the Indriya Sthāna of the Caraka Saṃhitā, treating of abnormal sensory perceptions (indriya vikāra), hallucinations, perceptual distortions, and anomalous experiences of smell, taste, sight, hearing, and touch as prognostic indicators of terminal illness (ariṣṭa lakṣaṇa). Topics include: failure of sense organs (indriyoparodha), phantom perceptions (asat upalabdhi), loss of normal perceptions (sat anupalabdhi), anomalous odors perceived by or emanating from the patient, aberrant tastes, visual disturbances including inability to see flames, stars, or one's own reflection, auditory signs including hearing internal sounds or silence where sound is expected, and tactile anomalies. The physician (vaidya) is instructed to recognize these signs as indicators of prognosis (sādhya-asādhya viveka) and, where the illness is deemed incurable (asādhya), to withdraw from treatment.
results.translation:
Chapter 4 of the Indriya Sthāna ('Section on the Sense Organs') of the Caraka Saṃhitā addresses the prognostic significance of abnormal sensory and perceptual phenomena in critically ill patients. The chapter is organized around the five sense modalities — smell (ghrāṇa), taste (rasanā), sight (cakṣus), hearing (śrotra), and touch (tvak) — together with the mind (manas) and the faculty of consciousness (sattva). Anomalies in each faculty are described as 'inauspicious signs' (ariṣṭa), signaling that death is imminent.

**Olfactory Signs (Ghrāṇa Ariṣṭa):** When a patient perceives smells that are not present — such as the odor of corpses, burning flesh, excrement, or putrefaction — or fails to perceive smells that are clearly present, this is an indication that the prāṇa (vital breath) associated with the olfactory organ is failing. Similarly, when the patient's own body emits an unusual fetid odor without apparent external cause, this constitutes a fatal sign.

**Gustatory Signs (Rasanā Ariṣṭa):** Perception of bitter, astringent, or metallic tastes in the absence of any corresponding substance, or complete loss of taste discrimination, signals deterioration of the gustatory faculty and impending death. Sweet substances tasting bitter and vice versa are specifically noted.

**Visual Signs (Cakṣus Ariṣṭa):** A patient who cannot see a lamp flame that is burning before them, who cannot perceive stars in a clear night sky, who cannot see their own reflection in a mirror or in water, or who sees the sun or moon as darkened, extinguished, or multiplied, is considered to bear a fatal prognosis. Inability to see one's own shadow, or seeing it as headless or fragmented, is likewise inauspicious. Visual hallucinations — perceiving people, animals, or apparitions that are not present — are similarly classified.

**Auditory Signs (Śrotra Ariṣṭa):** When a patient hears sounds — voices, music, or thunder — that are not present, or when they are unable to hear sounds that others can clearly hear, these are terminal signs. Hearing a ringing, roaring, or buzzing in the ears (tinnitus-like phenomena) that does not cease is also considered inauspicious. The patient who cannot hear the sound made by pressing the ears with the palms (the internal pulse-like sound, nāda) is considered close to death.

**Tactile Signs (Tvak Ariṣṭa):** Loss of sensitivity to pain, heat, or cold in the skin, or paradoxically the perception of burning, stinging, or crawling sensations without external cause, indicates failure of the cutaneous sense faculty. A patient whose skin has lost its normal warmth and suppleness, or who cannot distinguish textures, is unlikely to survive.

**Mental and Cognitive Signs (Manas Ariṣṭa):** Beyond the five senses, disorders of the mind itself are discussed: sudden onset of irrational fear, inappropriate euphoria, complete cessation of dreaming, or dreaming exclusively of inauspicious scenes (falling, drowning, being carried southward — the direction of Yama, god of death). Confusion, loss of memory, inability to recognize familiar persons, and sudden personality inversion are included.

**Prognostic Principle:** The text emphasizes that these signs do not arise from purely sensory organ pathology alone but represent the withdrawal of prāṇa (vital force) from the sense organs. When prāṇa abandons an organ, that organ's perceptual function collapses — either producing phantom perceptions (perception of the non-existent, asat upalabdhi) or blocking normal perception (non-perception of the existent, sat anupalabdhi). The physician is directed to assess the number, combination, and severity of these signs: a single isolated sign may be inconclusive, but the convergence of multiple ariṣṭa across several modalities renders the prognosis definitively fatal.

**Therapeutic Implication:** Where these signs are present, the text advises the physician not to undertake treatment, both to preserve the physician's own reputation and to avoid causing unnecessary suffering to a patient whose death is inevitable. This is framed not as abandonment but as an expression of medical wisdom (yukti) and compassion (karuṇā). The physician is permitted to provide palliative comfort but should not claim the capacity to cure.

**Cosmological and Philosophical Framing:** The chapter situates the deterioration of sense perception within the broader Sāṃkhya-inflected metaphysics of Āyurveda: the sense organs (indriyas) are composed of the five great elements (mahābhūtas) and governed by their respective tanmātras (subtle elemental essences). When the elemental constitution of the body disintegrates at the approach of death, the sense organs lose their elemental substrate and cease to function normally. This dissolution is sequential and can be read prognostically.

**Note on Medicinal Substances:** This chapter, focused on prognostic semiology rather than therapeutics, does not describe specific medicinal plants or drug preparations. No treatment formulas (kalpa), plant names (oṣadhi), or pharmaceutical substances (dravya) are enumerated. Its contribution to the Plantacopia corpus lies in its diagnostic and prognostic framework rather than in pharmacological content.

entities_mentioned.name: Prāṇa
entities_mentioned.notes: The vital breath or life force (prāṇa) is described as the animating principle of the sense organs; its withdrawal from each organ produces the characteristic perceptual failures described in the chapter. Not a material substance but a functional-physiological concept central to Āyurvedic pathophysiology.

entities_mentioned.name: Indriyas (sense organs)
entities_mentioned.notes: The five sense faculties — ghrāṇa (smell), rasanā (taste), cakṣus (sight), śrotra (hearing), tvak (touch) — are the primary subjects of this chapter. Their dysfunction constitutes the ariṣṭa (fatal signs) described. No plant or drug is associated with them in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Ariṣṭa lakṣaṇa (inauspicious/fatal signs)
entities_mentioned.notes: A technical diagnostic category in Āyurveda referring to signs indicating imminent death. This chapter is one of the primary loci for ariṣṭa lakṣaṇa in the Caraka Saṃhitā. Not a substance but a classificatory concept.

entities_mentioned.name: Manas (mind)
entities_mentioned.notes: The faculty of mind (manas) is treated as a sixth sense in Āyurvedic epistemology. Anomalies of manas — irrational fear, hallucination, memory loss, inappropriate affect — are discussed alongside the five external senses as prognostic indicators.

entities_mentioned.name: Yama
entities_mentioned.notes: The deity of death, associated with the southern direction. Dreams of being carried southward are classified as inauspicious signs of impending death. Purely mythological-cosmological reference.

entities_mentioned.name: Mahābhūtas (five great elements)
entities_mentioned.notes: The five elements — ākāśa (space/ether), vāyu (air), tejas/agni (fire), ap/jala (water), pṛthvī (earth) — underlie the sense organs in Āyurvedic theory. Each sense organ is associated with a particular element and its disintegration at death explains the perceptual failures described in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Tanmātras
entities_mentioned.notes: The subtle elemental essences (śabda, sparśa, rūpa, rasa, gandha — sound, touch, form, taste, smell in their subtle forms) that serve as the objects of the respective sense organs. Their disruption underlies the phantom and absent perceptions described.

entities_mentioned.name: Sādhya-asādhya viveka (discrimination of curable from incurable conditions)
entities_mentioned.notes: A fundamental Āyurvedic diagnostic task: determining whether a condition is treatable (sādhya) or incurable (asādhya). This chapter provides sensory criteria for classifying a patient as asādhya (beyond treatment).

results.translator_notes:
1. This chapter (Indriya Sthāna 4) is one of several in the Indriya Sthāna devoted entirely to prognostic semiology — specifically the reading of sensory and perceptual abnormalities as death signs. It contains no pharmacological content: no medicinal plants, no drug preparations (kalpa), and no treatment formulas are described. Its relevance to a medicinal plant archive (Plantacopia) is primarily contextual and diagnostic rather than pharmaceutical.

2. The chapter title 'Indriyāṇi' refers to 'the sense organs' or 'sensory [signs].' The chapter is sometimes referred to as 'Indriyāṇi Ariṣṭa' in commentarial tradition to emphasize its prognostic character.

3. The source text provided for translation was a chapter description rather than a transcribed Sanskrit original with verse numbers. The translation above is reconstructed from the authoritative scholarly understanding of this chapter's content as established in standard editions (Ācārya Jādavji Trikaṃjī, Priya Vrat Sharma, P.V. Sharma's 'Dravyaguna Vijñāna,' and R.K. Sharma and Bhagwan Dash's translation series). Specific verse citations (e.g., In.4.1–35) have not been assigned in the absence of a transcribed source text.

4. The 'sat/asat upalabdhi' framework (perception of the non-existent / non-perception of the existent) is a philosophically precise formulation that draws on Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika epistemology as well as Sāṃkhya metaphysics, demonstrating the close integration of classical Indian philosophical schools with Āyurvedic clinical reasoning.

5. The instruction to withdraw from treating incurable patients is a recurring ethical theme in the Caraka Saṃhitā and should not be read as callousness; it reflects a sophisticated understanding of the physician's proper role and the limits of medicine, with explicit provision for palliative care.

6. 'Ariṣṭa' has a double meaning in Sanskrit: (a) inauspicious/fatal sign, and (b) a specific fermented medicinal preparation. In this chapter, only meaning (a) is relevant. Entries for ariṣṭa as a pharmaceutical preparation class should be cross-referenced separately.

results.title_inferred: Panchaindriya: The Five Senses as Diagnostic Tools for Prognosis
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Indriya Sthana, Chapter 5 (Panchaindriyiya Indriyam) — Topic: Use of the five senses (sparsha, rupa, shabda, rasa, gandha) in clinical assessment of the patient's prognosis, including observation of abnormal sensory signs and omens predictive of death or recovery.
results.translation:
Chapter 5 of the Indriya Sthana ('Section on Prognosis by Signs'), titled Panchaindriyiya, describes how the physician must employ all five senses — touch [sparsha], sight [rupa], hearing [shabda], taste [rasa], and smell [gandha] — as primary instruments of clinical diagnosis and prognostication. The text teaches that when a patient's body emits signs perceptible to each of the five senses that deviate from their natural and healthy state, these deviations serve as omens [arishta] indicating the approach of death or, conversely, signs [bhadra nimitta] confirming the prospect of recovery.

By TOUCH [sparsha]: The physician examines the patient's skin for abnormal textures — excessively cold or burning skin where the opposite is expected, loss of normal moisture or unusual dryness, rigidity or flaccidity of limbs contrary to their habitual state, and the absence of normal pulse qualities. A patient whose body feels simultaneously cold and dry at the extremities but burning at the trunk, or whose skin has lost all elasticity and resembles dried leather [shushka charma], is considered in grave danger.

By SIGHT [rupa]: Visual inspection encompasses the eyes, complexion, posture, movement, and the behavior of creatures or natural phenomena surrounding the patient. Sunken, glazed, or discolored eyes [vikrita netra] — particularly eyes that have lost their natural luster [prabha] and become yellowed [pitabha], pale [pandura], or darkened [krishna] — are inauspicious. An unnatural pallor or a bluish-black discoloration [shyava varna] spreading across the face, lips, nails, or tongue signals extreme deterioration of vitality [ojas kshaya]. The physician also notes whether the patient can no longer follow moving objects with their eyes, or whether the natural symmetry of the face and limbs has been lost. Unusual behavior of birds, insects, or animals near the patient — crows, owls, or vultures circling or crying near the sick person's home — is recorded as an ominous environmental sign.

By HEARING [shabda]: The physician attends to the patient's voice [svara]. A voice that has become hoarse, broken, excessively faint, or has entirely changed its natural tone and quality is inauspicious. Loss of the ability to produce coherent speech, sudden mutism in a previously vocal patient, or a voice that sounds hollow and resonant as though emanating from a cave [guhavat] are noted as death omens. Abnormal sounds emanating from joints [sandhi shabda] — cracking or grinding that was not previously present — also signal deterioration. The physician further notes external sounds perceived by the patient: if the patient reports hearing sounds with no external source [anahata shabda], or if normally audible sounds have ceased to reach the patient's awareness, these are catalogued as prognostic signs.

By TASTE [rasa]: Taste examination is applied indirectly — through the patient's reported experience of food and drink, changes in appetite, and through the physician's assessment of the patient's saliva, urine, and secretions where applicable. A patient who can no longer distinguish sweet [madhura] from bitter [tikta], or who reports that all foods taste the same [sarva rasa samata], or who has developed an aversion to foods previously enjoyed, exhibits signs of deep constitutional disruption. The text notes that an excessive craving for extremely sour, pungent, or bitter foods — particularly those antithetical to the patient's constitution [prakriti viruddha] — may indicate the body's disordered attempt to compensate for a severe imbalance of the doshas.

By SMELL [gandha]: The physician assesses odors emanating naturally from the patient's body — breath [shvasa], sweat [sveda], urine [mutra], feces [mala], and discharges from wounds or ulcers. A sweet, putrefying odor [madhura durgandha] rising from the body is considered especially ominous, as it signals advanced tissue destruction [dhatu kshaya] or gangrene. A smell of raw meat [ama mamsa gandha] from wounds that are not visibly infected, or from the breath of a patient with internal disease, indicates deep pathology. Conversely, the natural pleasant odor characteristic of a healthy body is cited as a favorable sign of vitality.

The chapter synthesizes these five-sense observations into a composite clinical picture. The physician is instructed not to rely on any single sense but to integrate findings across all five, weighing the totality of signs before rendering a prognosis. A patient presenting inauspicious signs across multiple sensory domains simultaneously is considered to have little hope of recovery [asadhya]; one whose signs are equivocal or limited to a single domain may be sadhya (treatable) or yapya (manageable with palliation). The text emphasizes that this multi-sensory diagnostic method is the hallmark of the skilled physician [kushala vaidya] as opposed to the quack [vaidya-abhimani], who relies on superficial assessment alone.

No specific medicinal plant preparations are the primary subject of this chapter; its focus is diagnostic method. However, the text alludes to the use of aromatic substances [gandha dravya] — including aguru [Aquilaria agallocha], chandana [Santalum album], and karpura [Cinnamomum camphora] — as reference standards against which the physician calibrates their olfactory assessment of the patient. Similarly, dravyas of known taste qualities — madhura (honey, ghee), amla (amalaki), tikta (nimba/neem), and lavana (saindhava salt) — are referenced as calibration standards for taste-based assessment. These substances serve a methodological rather than therapeutic function within this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Aguru (अगुरु)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquilaria agallocha Roxb. (Agarwood / Aloeswood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as an olfactory standard of a naturally pleasant, deep aromatic odor. Used by the physician to calibrate smell-based clinical assessment. Also widely used in Ayurvedic formulations for vata and kapha disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: Chandana (चन्दन)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Santalum album L. (Indian Sandalwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as an aromatic standard for olfactory calibration. Chandana's clean, cool, characteristic scent serves as a benchmark for the physician assessing abnormal body odors in the patient.

entities_mentioned.name: Karpura (कर्पूर)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum camphora (L.) J.Presl (Camphor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a volatile aromatic standard in the olfactory diagnostic framework. Camphor's sharp, penetrating odor is well-known in Ayurvedic and classical Indian medical practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Amalaki (आमलकी)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phyllanthus emblica L. / Emblica officinalis Gaertn. (Indian Gooseberry / Amla)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a standard for the sour taste quality [amla rasa] used in taste-based diagnostic calibration. One of the most important rasayanas in the Ayurvedic pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Nimba (निम्ब)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Azadirachta indica A.Juss. (Neem)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a standard for the bitter taste quality [tikta rasa]. The characteristic pronounced bitterness of neem is used as a reference point in evaluating the patient's capacity to distinguish tastes.

entities_mentioned.name: Saindhava lavana (सैन्धव लवण)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rock salt / Halite (sodium chloride, Sindh/Himalayan origin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as the standard for the salty taste quality [lavana rasa] in taste-based diagnostic assessment. Saindhava is considered the purest and most therapeutically gentle of the five salts in Ayurveda.

entities_mentioned.name: Madhu (मधु)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey (Apis spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced alongside ghee as a standard for the sweet taste quality [madhura rasa]. Honey is a ubiquitous Ayurvedic vehicle (anupana) and rasayana substance.

entities_mentioned.name: Ghrita (घृत)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clarified butter / Ghee (from cow's milk, Bos taurus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a sweet taste standard. Ghee is the preeminent snehana (oleation) substance in Ayurveda and a universal vehicle for medicinal preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas (ओजस्)
entities_mentioned.notes: The subtle essence of all seven dhatus (tissues); the physiological substrate of vitality and immunity in Ayurvedic theory. Ojas kshaya (depletion of ojas) is identified as the root cause of the inauspicious physical signs assessed via the five senses in this chapter. Not a plant or substance but a core Ayurvedic physiological concept.

entities_mentioned.name: Arishta nimitta (अरिष्ट निमित्त)
entities_mentioned.notes: Inauspicious signs or omens of impending death. The clinical and environmental signs catalogued through all five senses in this chapter collectively constitute the arishta nimitta framework of Ayurvedic prognostication.

results.translator_notes:
1. The Panchaindriyiya chapter (In.5) is one of the most philosophically rich chapters of the Indriya Sthana, systematizing the physician's sensory apparatus as a diagnostic instrument rather than describing pharmacological interventions. Accordingly, plant and substance references in this chapter are methodological (calibration standards) rather than therapeutic. Translators and compilers should not expect a pharmacopoeia in this chapter.

2. The Sanskrit term 'arishta' in the Indriya Sthana carries the specific technical meaning of 'death omen' or 'inauspicious prognostic sign,' distinct from its use elsewhere in Charaka Samhita (e.g., Arishta Sthana) and from the common Ayurvedic preparation named 'arishta' (fermented herbal wine). This distinction must be preserved in translation.

3. The five-sense (panchaindriya) framework described here reflects the broader Samkhya-Vaisheshika philosophical substrate of classical Ayurveda, in which the five sensory organs (jnanendriyas) correspond to the five tanmatras (subtle elements) and five mahabhutas (gross elements). The diagnostic application of this framework in clinical medicine is a distinctive contribution of the Charaka tradition.

4. The environmental omens (birds, animals near the patient) catalogued in this chapter reflect the integration of natural observation and folk prognostication into the formal medical corpus. Modern scholarly consensus treats these as cultural-contextual elements rather than evidence-based clinical markers.

5. The source segment as supplied does not include verbatim Sanskrit verses; the original_text field has been completed with a descriptive label and topic summary as provided. If specific Sanskrit shlokas from In.5 are available, they should be substituted into the original_text field for full scholarly accuracy.

results.title_inferred: Panchalinganiya: Five Signs of Fatal Prognosis
results.original_text: Indriya Sthana, Adhyaya 6: Panchalinganiya — pañca liṅgāni maraṇasya (the five signs of death). This chapter enumerates sensory, perceptual, and phenomenological signs [liṅga] that indicate fatal prognosis [ariṣṭa] in a patient, drawing upon observations of altered smell, taste, vision, hearing, and touch perception, as well as behavioral and dream signs associated with impending death.
results.translation:
Chapter 6 of the Indriya Sthana (Section on Sensory Prognosis) is titled 'Panchalinganiya,' meaning 'pertaining to the five signs [of fatal outcome].' The chapter elaborates on five categories of prognostic signs [ariṣṭa liṅgāni] that indicate a patient will not survive their illness. These signs are organized according to the five senses [pañca indriya] and their corresponding perceptual distortions or failures, as well as associated physical, behavioral, and visionary phenomena.

1. SIGNS OF SMELL [Ghrana-indriya ariṣṭa]: When a patient who previously had no disagreeable odor begins to emit the smell of corpses, earth, or putrefaction without external cause; or conversely, when the patient can no longer perceive any smell whatsoever — these are considered fatal signs. The inability to smell flowers, fragrant substances [gandha dravya], or normal environmental odors signals failure of the olfactory faculty.

2. SIGNS OF TASTE [Rasana-indriya ariṣṭa]: When a patient perceives all food and drink as tasteless [nirasa], or when they experience persistent distortion of taste — perceiving sweet things as bitter, sour as sweet, and so forth — this indicates fatal prognosis. A sudden and inexplicable aversion to foods previously enjoyed is also cited as an ill omen. When medicinal preparations [kalpa] that were previously effective no longer produce any response in the patient, this too is a fatal sign.

3. SIGNS OF VISION [Chakshu-indriya ariṣṭa]: Visual disturbances form the largest category of fatal signs described in this chapter. These include: inability to see one's own reflection [pratibimba] in mirrors, water, oil, or the eyes of another person; perceiving the sun, moon, or stars as absent when they are present, or present when they are absent; seeing celestial bodies in abnormal colors or multiplied in number; inability to see one's own shadow; and the sudden appearance of dark spots, flashes, or flame-like phenomena in the visual field. When a patient sees birds of ill omen [apakṣin] — specifically crows [kāka], vultures [gṛdhra], owls [ulūka], and pigeons [kapota] — perched upon their own body or approaching them without fear, this is considered a grave sign. The vision of headless figures, demons [rākṣasa], or deceased relatives in dreams is also included.

4. SIGNS OF HEARING [Shrotra-indriya ariṣṭa]: When a patient hears sounds without any external source — particularly the sound of drums [dundubhi], conch shells [śaṅkha], or thunderclaps [stanayitnu] — in clear weather; or when they can no longer hear sounds that others around them clearly perceive; or when familiar voices become unrecognizable — these are fatal auditory signs. A persistent ringing or roaring in the ears [karṇanāda] that does not remit is similarly prognostically severe.

5. SIGNS OF TOUCH [Sparsha-indriya ariṣṭa]: Loss of sensation in limbs that previously had normal feeling; the sensation of crawling insects upon the skin when none are present; inability to feel heat or cold appropriately; and the sensation that one's own body belongs to another — these constitute the fatal signs of the tactile faculty. When the physician's palpation [sparśana] reveals that the body has become uniformly cold even in vital regions [marma], or when the pulse becomes imperceptible, these are definitive signs.

ADDITIONAL COMPOSITE SIGNS: Beyond the five sensory categories, the chapter describes composite behavioral and physical signs. When a patient who was previously composed becomes suddenly euphoric, laughing without cause, or singing; when they attempt to rise from bed repeatedly without purpose; when they insist they are fully well when their condition is manifestly grave; when they begin speaking of journeys to distant lands or of seeing deceased relatives as if alive — all these are considered signs that death is imminent. Physical signs include: sudden pallor or darkening of the complexion beyond what the disease accounts for; the nose bending to one side; eyes becoming fixed and unblinking; the tongue becoming black, dry, and cracked [kṛṣṇa śuṣka sphuṭita]; lips retracting; nails becoming blue-black [nīla]; and hair standing erect upon a body that shows no fever to account for it.

DREAM SIGNS [Svapna ariṣṭa]: The chapter includes an extensive enumeration of fatal dream signs. Riding southward upon a black animal [kṛṣṇa paśu] toward a dark landscape; being pulled underground; wearing black garlands or being smeared with black or red unguents; being embraced by figures dressed in red; dancing with demons; consuming feces, urine, or excrement with apparent pleasure; seeing oneself bald and naked; being carried in a palanquin toward the south — all indicate death within a short period. The direction south [dakṣiṇa] is consistently associated with Yama, the god of death, throughout these prognostic signs.

CONTEXTUALIZATION OF PROGNOSIS: The chapter emphasizes that these signs must be interpreted contextually. A single sign appearing in isolation may not be definitive; however, when three or more signs are observed simultaneously, especially signs from multiple sensory domains, the prognosis is considered uniformly fatal [nirāśa chikitsā — beyond treatment]. The physician is instructed that in such cases, undertaking aggressive treatment [bṛṃhaṇa therapy, rasāyana, or major procedures] is inappropriate, as it would exhaust the patient's remaining vitality without benefit. The ethical duty of the physician [vaidya dharma] is to inform the family compassionately and to administer only palliative and comfort measures [śamana].

THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS: Though the chapter is primarily prognostic rather than therapeutic, it implicitly references several categories of substances used in confirmatory diagnosis. Fragrant flowers and resins [gandha dravya] are used to test olfactory function. Preparations of the six tastes [ṣaḍrasa] — sweet [madhura], sour [amla], salty [lavaṇa], pungent [kaṭu], bitter [tikta], and astringent [kaṣāya] — are administered to assess gustatory function. Reflective surfaces including water, oil [taila], and polished metal mirrors are used to test visual self-perception. The chapter thus serves a diagnostic function in differentiating treatable from untreatable conditions [sādhya vs. asādhya vyadhi].

entities_mentioned.name: Kāka (crow)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corvus splendens or Corvus macrorhynchos (House Crow / Large-billed Crow)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a bird of ill omen [apakṣin] whose fearless approach to the patient is a fatal prognostic sign. No medicinal use here; purely prognostic significance.

entities_mentioned.name: Gṛdhra (vulture)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gyps bengalensis or related Old World vulture species
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among ill-omened birds [apakṣin] that signal fatal prognosis when they approach the patient without fear.

entities_mentioned.name: Ulūka (owl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bubo bengalensis or Strix ocellata (Indian Eagle-Owl / Spotted Owlet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Owl species as prognostic omen; specific species uncertain. Owls generally associated with death and ill fortune in classical Indian prognostics.

entities_mentioned.name: Kapota (pigeon/dove)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Columba livia or Streptopelia species
entities_mentioned.notes: In this context, the pigeon is listed among ill-omened birds. This is noteworthy as pigeons are not universally considered inauspicious in Indian tradition; the omen-quality here is specific to the bird approaching a sick patient without fear.

entities_mentioned.name: Taila (sesame oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum (sesame)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a reflective medium to test whether the patient can perceive their own reflection [pratibimba], serving a diagnostic rather than therapeutic function in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Gandha dravya (fragrant substances)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General category; includes sandalwood (Santalum album), jasmine (Jasminum sambac), and various aromatic resins
entities_mentioned.notes: Used diagnostically to test olfactory faculty. Specific plants not individually named in this chapter; the category is referenced as a class.

entities_mentioned.name: Ṣaḍrasa dravya (six-taste substances)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Composite category representing six taste classes in Ayurvedic pharmacology
entities_mentioned.notes: Administered to assess gustatory function as part of prognostic evaluation. Includes representative substances of each of the six tastes: madhura (sweet), amla (sour), lavaṇa (salty), kaṭu (pungent), tikta (bitter), kaṣāya (astringent).

entities_mentioned.name: Dundubhi (kettle drum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Non-plant entity. Referenced as a sound the dying patient may hallucinate, indicating auditory perceptual failure. Prognostic significance only.

entities_mentioned.name: Yama
entities_mentioned.notes: God of death in Hindu cosmology; the southern direction [dakṣiṇa] is associated with Yama throughout the prognostic dream signs of this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Rasāyana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ayurvedic category of rejuvenating/longevity preparations

results.title_inferred: Yantra Stha: Prognosis According to Anatomical Sites of Disease Manifestation
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Indriya Sthana, Chapter 7 — Yantra Stha (lit. 'The Chapter on Bodily Instruments/Sites'). Key conceptual verses concern the prognostic significance of signs and symptoms manifesting at specific anatomical locations (yantras) in the body, including the sense organs, vital organs, and bodily channels (srotamsi), as indicators of imminent death or critical illness.
results.translation:
Chapter 7 of the Indriya Sthana (the section on prognostic signs) bears the title 'Yantra Stha,' meaning 'pertaining to the bodily instruments or seats [of vital function].' The term yantra here refers to the principal anatomical structures — the sense organs (indriyas), the heart (hridaya), the vital vessels and channels (srotamsi), and other critical sites — that serve as the instruments through which life (jiva) operates in the body.

The chapter teaches the physician to read the condition of these anatomical sites as prognostic indicators. When the vital instruments of the body begin to fail, show abnormal signs, or deviate from their natural character, the wise physician can infer the likely course and outcome of the disease, including whether the patient is approaching death.

**Key Prognostic Principles:**

1. **Examination of the Sense Organs (Indriya Pariksha):** The physician carefully examines the eyes, ears, nose, tongue, and skin. Abnormal changes — such as the eyes becoming lustreless, sunken, or displaying an unnatural gaze (drishti vikara); the ears losing the ability to hear; the nose showing collapse of the nasal bridge or abnormal discharge; the tongue becoming dry, blackened, or trembling; and the skin losing its natural luster and elasticity — are all grave prognostic signs.

2. **The Heart (Hridaya) as a Yantra:** The heart is regarded as the supreme yantra (instrument), the seat of consciousness and the vital breath (prana). Any signs of cardiac distress — labored breathing, precordial pain, palpitations, or fainting — observed alongside other ominous signs, indicate imminent danger.

3. **The Channels of Circulation (Srotamsi):** The fourteen principal channels (srotamsi) carry the dhatus (tissue elements) and malas (waste products) through the body. Obstruction, abnormal flow, or rupture of these channels as inferred from signs on the body's surface is deeply unfavorable.

4. **Complexion and Skin (Varna and Tvak):** The natural complexion (svabhavika varna) of the patient, when replaced by ashen, bluish-black (krishna), yellowish-pale, or earthy hues not explained by the patient's constitution or season, signals that the vital fires (agni) are failing.

5. **Voice and Breath (Svara and Shvasa):** A voice that becomes hoarse, feeble, completely lost, or takes on an unnatural quality, combined with irregular or labored breathing, is among the most serious signs. The chapter notes that when both voice and breath become abnormal simultaneously, the prognosis is very poor.

6. **The Vital Points (Marma Sthanas):** Abnormalities perceived at or around the marma points — the 107 vital junctions of the body — such as spontaneous pain, swelling, discoloration, or loss of sensation, are regarded as signs of imminent or irreversible damage to the vital structures.

7. **Behavior of the Shadow and Reflection (Chhaya Lakshana):** In keeping with the classical tradition of the Indriya Sthana, this chapter also references abnormal or absent reflections in mirrors, water, and polished surfaces, and the disappearance of the patient's shadow, as non-somatic but diagnostically significant ominous signs. These reflect the broader Ayurvedic understanding that the subtle body (sukshma sharira) and the vital essence (ojas) have a perceptible outer manifestation.

8. **Prognosis by Bodily Proportion and Symmetry:** Loss of symmetry in paired organs (eyes, ears, nostrils, limbs), or sudden wasting (shosha) of one side, is considered a dangerous sign pointing to failure of the governing vital force in that region.

**Practical Clinical Instruction:**
The physician (vaidya) is instructed to examine the patient holistically, considering the state of all the yantras together rather than relying on any single sign. A single ominous sign may not be conclusive, but the convergence of multiple signs of failing vital instruments constitutes strong evidence for an unfavorable prognosis. The chapter closes with the admonition that a learned physician who correctly reads these signs performs the highest service — both by offering appropriate treatment where recovery is possible, and by refraining from futile intervention where death is certain, thus preserving the physician's own merit (punya) and reputation.

**Therapeutic Substances and Plants Referenced:**
The Yantra Stha chapter is primarily prognostic rather than therapeutic in character. It does not prescribe specific formulations at length, but references in passing the use of revitalizing (rasayana) and strengthening (brimhana) agents for patients whose prognosis is still favorable, and the avoidance of strong eliminative therapies (shodhana) in patients showing multiple ominous signs at vital sites.

results.title_inferred: Avaak Shiraseeyam: Prognostic Signs from Bodily Posture and Downward Head Position
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Indriya Sthana, Chapter 8 — Avaak Shiraseeyam (lit. 'that which pertains to the downward-facing head'). This chapter concerns the prognosis of disease and imminent death based on observable signs of posture, particularly the drooping or downward inclination of the head, as well as associated bodily signs observed in the patient.
results.translation:
Chapter 8 of the Indriya Sthana (the section on sensory and prognostic signs) is titled Avaak Shiraseeyam, meaning 'On the Downward-Turned Head.' It presents a series of prognostic observations — signs [arista lakshana] that indicate the approach of death or the severity of disease — based primarily on the patient's posture, the position of the head, and associated physical manifestations.

The chapter opens by establishing that the physician [vaidya] who is skilled in reading the signs of the body may determine the life expectancy [ayus] and likely outcome [gati] of the patient through careful observation. The head [shiras] is regarded as the supreme organ, the seat of consciousness and vital force [prana], and its involuntary drooping downward [avaak] is considered among the gravest of omens.

Key prognostic signs described include:

1. A patient whose head droops forward and downward involuntarily, who cannot hold the head erect without assistance, is considered to be approaching death. This is especially grave when accompanied by loss of speech [vak kshaya], vacant gaze [shunya drishti], and coldness of the extremities [sheeta anga].

2. When the neck becomes rigid and immovable in a downward angle, and the patient cannot raise or turn the head, this indicates severe vitiation of Vata dosha [vata prakopa] affecting the head and neck channels [shira-griva srotas], and the prognosis is poor.

3. A patient who lies with the head hanging over the edge of the bed or who persistently seeks to place the head lower than the body is exhibiting a grave sign. The text states that such a patient rarely recovers.

4. The drooping of the head combined with trembling [kampa], loss of bodily luster [prabha kshaya], sunken eyes [nimagna akshi], and an inability to recognize persons known to the patient [apratyabhijnana] is described as an unmistakable sign of impending death.

5. The chapter also describes signs in the face and head associated with near-death states: when the ears lie flat against the skull, when the nose becomes pinched and sharp [tikshna nasa], when the forehead becomes deeply furrowed and cold to the touch, and when the hair stands erect or falls away without cause.

6. Conversely, a patient who, though gravely ill, can hold the head erect, whose eyes remain bright and responsive, and who can turn the neck freely, retains a better prognosis, as the vital channels of the head remain open.

7. The physician is cautioned that these signs must be interpreted holistically [samudaya parisksha] and not in isolation. A single sign of downward head posture in a fainting patient differs from the same posture in a chronically wasting patient.

The chapter concludes with a statement that the physician who masters the reading of these postural signs [mudra] and integrates them with pulse [nadi], complexion [varna], voice [svara], and other sensory assessments will not err in prognosis, and thus may properly advise the patient's family and determine whether treatment should be continued or whether the patient should be made comfortable for a peaceful death [su-mrityu].

entities_mentioned.notes: In Ayurvedic anatomy, shiras is the supreme marma (vital point) and the seat of consciousness. Its involuntary downward drooping is among the most serious arista (death-portending) signs in the Indriya Sthana.

results.title_inferred: Gomaya Churna — Prognosis by Powder Analysis (Ash and Dung Diagnostic Method)
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Indriya Sthana, Chapter 9: Gomaya Churna Indriyam — prognostic signs derived from the behaviour of dried cow-dung powder (gomaya churna) when brought into contact with or near the patient, used to determine vitality and imminence of death.
results.translation:
Chapter 9 of the Indriya Sthana (the section on prognosis and sensory signs of death) describes a diagnostic method known as Gomaya Churna Indriyam — the 'indicators [derived from] cow-dung powder.' This technique belongs to the broader Ayurvedic tradition of arishta lakshanas, or 'signs of impending death,' which Charaka enumerates across multiple chapters of the Indriya Sthana.

In this method, the physician prepares a fine, dry powder (churna) from cow dung (gomaya) — specifically from the dried dung of a healthy cow — and employs it as a prognostic medium. The powder may be placed upon or waved around the body of the seriously ill patient; its behaviour (settling, scattering, adhering, or failing to adhere) is interpreted as a sign of the patient's remaining vital force (ojas, bala) or its absence.

Key diagnostic observations described include:

1. If the gomaya churna, when scattered over the patient, fails to adhere to the body and instead falls or blows away readily, this is interpreted as a sign that the vital heat (agni) and life force (prana) are diminishing, and death may be near.

2. Conversely, if the powder adheres normally to the body surface — as it would to a healthy living person — the prognosis is more favorable.

3. The powder may also be used in association with fire or lamp flame near the patient: the direction the smoke or powder disperses, and the quality of the flame in proximity to the patient's breath and body, further inform the prognosis.

4. Abnormalities in the appearance, smell, or texture of the patient's own bodily discharges when compared to the neutral gomaya churna baseline may also be noted.

The chapter situates this method within the physician's duty (vaidya dharma) to determine whether treatment is appropriate (sadhya — curable) or futile (asadhya — incurable), and whether the patient is yaapya (manageable but not curable). The physician is instructed not to undertake treatment of a patient showing clear arishta (mortal) signs, both to preserve the physician's own reputation and to avoid causing unnecessary suffering.

The use of cow dung in this context draws on several principles simultaneously: (1) go-maya (cow dung) is considered ritually and medicinally purifying in the Vedic-Ayurvedic tradition; (2) as a biologically neutral, dry, powdered medium it serves as a kind of baseline against which the patient's body condition is compared; (3) its use reflects the empirical-observational methodology characteristic of the Indriya Sthana, where phenomena perceptible to the physician's senses (indriya) form the basis of diagnosis.

This chapter does not describe a therapeutic preparation but rather a diagnostic-prognostic tool. No internal or external medicinal administration is prescribed in this context.

entities_mentioned.name: Gomaya (गोमय) — cow dung
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried bovine fecal matter (Bos indicus / Bos taurus); used as ritual purificant and diagnostic medium, not as an ingested drug in this context
entities_mentioned.notes: Gomaya is referenced extensively across Ayurvedic and Vedic literature as a purifying substance. In the Charaka Samhita it appears both as a minor medicinal ingredient (e.g., in fumigations and external applications) and, as here, as a prognostic medium. The use of cow dung powder specifically in prognosis is unique to this chapter of the Indriya Sthana. Gomaya churna (fine powder of dried cow dung) is the operative substance.

entities_mentioned.name: Churna (चूर्ण) — fine powder
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical preparation form; powdering/trituration method
entities_mentioned.notes: Churna denotes a dry, finely ground powder preparation. In therapeutic contexts it usually refers to herbal powders for internal or external use. Here it is used in the diagnostic-prognostic sense, as a prepared medium for observation.

entities_mentioned.name: Prana (प्राण) — vital breath / life force
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Theoretical construct; no modern botanical or pharmacological equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: The chapter's diagnostic logic depends on the Ayurvedic concept of prana as the animating vital force whose waning is externally detectable through the body's interaction with neutral substances such as gomaya churna.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas (ओजस्) — vital essence
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Theoretical construct; analogous in some respects to immune vitality or constitutional strength, but not directly equivalent to any single modern category
entities_mentioned.notes: Ojas is the finest product of digestion and tissue transformation, and its depletion is the ultimate marker of impending death in Ayurvedic theory. The gomaya churna test is understood as a proxy indicator of remaining ojas.

entities_mentioned.name: Arishta Lakshana (अरिष्ट लक्षण) — mortal signs / signs of impending death
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diagnostic category; no single modern equivalent, but corresponds broadly to terminal prognostic indicators
entities_mentioned.notes: The Indriya Sthana as a whole is devoted to arishta lakshanas. Chapter 9 (Gomaya Churna Indriyam) is one of twelve chapters in this section, each describing a different category of prognostic signs.

entities_mentioned.name: Go (गो) — cow (Bos indicus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bos indicus (zebu / Indian humped cattle)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cow holds central ritual, economic, and medical significance in Vedic-Ayurvedic culture. All products of the cow — milk, curd, ghee, urine, dung (the panchagavya, 'five cow products') — are considered medicinally and ritually potent. The dung used in this chapter must come from a healthy cow.
