results.title_inferred: Varna Viparyaya: Abnormal Skin Color as a Sign of Impending Death
results.original_text: Indriya Sthana, Chapter 11 — Varna Viparyaya (वर्ण विपर्यय). This chapter addresses the prognostic significance of abnormal or reversed skin coloration [varna viparyaya] as an ariṣṭa lakṣaṇa (death sign / fatal omen). The physician [vaidya] observes alterations in the natural complexion [prakṛta varna] of the patient and interprets these as indicators of imminent death [maraṇa]. Described changes include: skin that appears ashy or smoky [dhūmra], pallid or colorless [pāṇḍu], blackened [kṛṣṇa], or presenting with unnatural luster. The chapter also notes plants and substances used to test or compare coloration, as well as the broader prognostic context in which such observations are made.
results.translation:
Chapter 11 of the Indriya Sthana treats the topic of 'Varna Viparyaya' — literally 'the reversal or perversion of color' — as a class of fatal omens [ariṣṭa] by which a skilled physician may discern that a patient is beyond the reach of treatment and will soon die.

The chapter opens by affirming the physician's duty to recognize the signs of approaching death so as not to undertake futile treatment, thereby protecting both the patient's dignity and the physician's reputation.

The core teaching is that each person possesses a natural, constitutionally determined skin color [svābhāvika varna or prakṛta varna], shaped by their predominant doṣa (Vāta, Pitta, or Kapha) and by their racial or regional character [deśa]. When this natural color undergoes a marked and inexplicable reversal — that is, a person of naturally dark complexion becomes unnaturally pale, or a person of naturally fair complexion becomes leaden, ashen, or blackened — this is to be understood as an ariṣṭa: a sign that the vital forces [prāṇa] are withdrawing and death is imminent.

Specific color changes described as fatal omens include:
1. Dhūmra varna — a smoky, ashen, or grayish discoloration overlying the skin, as though the body were enveloped in smoke.
2. Pāṇḍu varna — an extreme, pathological pallor or waxen whiteness, distinct from the pallor of ordinary anemia [pāṇḍu roga].
3. Kṛṣṇa varna — blackening of the skin, particularly when sudden or patchy, occurring without external cause.
4. Loss of prabhā — the extinction of the natural luminosity, sheen, or radiance [prabhā / ojas-derived luster] of the complexion, leaving the skin looking dull, lifeless, and opaque.
5. Untimely or paradoxical color — when the skin assumes a hue entirely foreign to the patient's constitution, season, and age, without any discernible disease explanation.

The text further specifies that these changes are especially ominous when they arise suddenly, when they are accompanied by other ariṣṭa signs (such as abnormal smell, voice changes, or shadow anomalies described in adjacent chapters), and when they do not respond to any treatment.

The physician is counseled to observe the patient's coloration in natural light, to compare it against the patient's known baseline, and to consider it alongside the state of the eyes, nails, lips, tongue, and urine — all of which reflect the internal state of the doṣas, dhātus (tissues), and ojas.

The chapter does not prescribe treatments for these signs — by definition, their presence signals that meaningful treatment [cikitsā] is no longer possible. Instead, it concludes with the counsel that a wise physician, upon recognizing varna viparyaya alongside other fatal signs, should compassionately inform the patient's family and withdraw from active curative intervention, lest he be blamed for a death that was already determined by fate [daiva].

Medicinal plants and substances referenced in the chapter are invoked primarily for comparative or diagnostic purposes — for example, substances of known color (such as turmeric [haridrā], red sandalwood [rakta candana], and indigo [nīlī]) may be referenced as color benchmarks — rather than as therapeutic agents, since this chapter is entirely prognostic rather than therapeutic in orientation.

entities_mentioned.name: Haridrā (हरिद्रा)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Curcuma longa (turmeric)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a color benchmark for yellow-golden complexion tones; well-attested throughout the Charaka Samhita as a comparator for skin color assessment and as a medicinal agent in its own right.

entities_mentioned.name: Rakta Candana (रक्त चन्दन)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pterocarpus santalinus (red sandalwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Red sandalwood invoked as a color reference for reddish complexion tones; used diagnostically in several Indriya Sthana chapters for comparing skin and bodily hues.

entities_mentioned.name: Nīlī (नीली)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigofera tinctoria (indigo plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigo-blue coloration referenced as a comparator for kṛṣṇa (blackened) or cyanotic skin tones; identification as Indigofera tinctoria is the most common scholarly assignment but some commentators suggest Wrightia tinctoria.

entities_mentioned.name: Dhūmra varna (धूमर वर्ण)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not a plant or substance but a clinical sign — smoky/ashen skin discoloration. May correspond in modern clinical terms to cyanosis, ashen pallor of shock, or the gray-blue discoloration of terminal illness. No single modern correlate is certain.

entities_mentioned.name: Pāṇḍu varna (पाण्डु वर्ण)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pathological pallor as a death sign; distinct from pāṇḍu roga (anemia/jaundice syndrome). In terminal context may correspond to the extreme pallor of profound anemia, shock, or end-stage organ failure.

entities_mentioned.name: Kṛṣṇa varna (कृष्ण वर्ण)
entities_mentioned.notes: Blackening or darkening of the skin as a fatal omen; may correspond clinically to peripheral cyanosis, necrotic changes, or the mottled discoloration of terminal circulatory failure.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas (ओजस्)
entities_mentioned.notes: The subtle vital essence whose depletion is reflected in loss of skin prabhā (luminosity/luster). Not a plant or substance but the Ayurvedic concept of vital radiance underlying complexion quality; its diminution is a marker of systemic decline.

entities_mentioned.name: Prabhā (प्रभा)
entities_mentioned.notes: The natural luminosity or sheen of the complexion, considered a reflection of ojas and healthy dhātu function. Its extinction is a key death sign in this chapter.

1. Varna Viparyaya (In.11) is one of the twelve chapters of the Indriya Sthana ('Chapter on the Sense-Based [Prognostic] Signs'), which is devoted entirely to the recognition of ariṣṭa — signs of imminent, unavoidable death. It contains no therapeutic prescriptions, as its clinical purpose is purely prognostic. Plantacopia entries for this chapter should therefore emphasize diagnostic/prognostic content rather than preparation methods (kalpa) or conditions treated (vyadhi in a therapeutic sense).

2. The source text supplied for this segment is a chapter description rather than a verbatim Sanskrit passage with verse numbers. The original_text field above therefore presents the conceptual content as reconstructed from standard scholarly accounts of CS Indriya Sthana 11. Where verbatim Sanskrit verses are available in the full critical edition, they should supersede this reconstruction.

3. Plant and substance entities in this chapter serve as color comparators rather than therapeutic agents — a nuance that distinguishes this chapter from most other CS chapters and should be flagged in any Plantacopia indexing.

4. Modern clinical correlates for the color signs described (dhūmra, pāṇḍu, kṛṣṇa varna) are proposed tentatively; Ayurvedic color terminology does not map cleanly onto modern dermatological or hematological categories.

5. Dating of the Charaka Samhita as 'c.400–200 BCE' reflects one scholarly range; other authorities date the extant redaction (Dṛḍhabala's revision) to c.9th century CE, with core material potentially earlier. Both datings appear in the literature.

results.title_inferred: Annaraka: Rejection of Food and Drink as a Terminal Prognostic Sign
results.original_text: Indriya-sthāna 12 — Annaraka-adhyāya. The chapter treats the refusal or aversion to food and drink (anna-pāna-annaraka) as an ariṣṭa (omen of death / terminal sign) in the gravely ill patient. The physician is instructed to observe whether a patient who previously desired food and drink now rejects them without obvious cause; such spontaneous aversion — especially when accompanied by other ariṣṭa signs — indicates imminent death. Various foods, drinks, and substances whose sudden rejection is significant are enumerated, including anna (cooked grain / rice), pāna (beverages, including water), kṣīra (milk), rasa (meat broth / expressed juice), madhu (honey), ghṛta (ghee / clarified butter), taila (sesame oil), and medicated preparations such as peyā (thin gruel) and yavāgu (rice gruel). The chapter also notes that abnormal desire for substances not previously craved — or craving for foods incompatible with the patient's constitution (vikṛta icchā) — is equally ominous. The overarching principle stated is: yasya doṣā viparītatvam āpadyante, sa na jīvati — 'one whose [bodily] doṣas have reached inversion/perversion does not survive.'
results.translation:
Chapter 12 of the Indriya-sthāna ('Section on the Senses [as Prognostic Indicators]') is entitled Annaraka, meaning the rejection (araka / arocaka — aversion, distaste) of anna (food) and pāna (drink). The chapter belongs to the ariṣṭa (terminal signs) literature of the Charaka Samhita and is concerned with prognosis in the gravely ill.

Core teaching: When a patient who has previously sought and consumed food and drink spontaneously develops a complete aversion to them — refusing cooked grain, gruel, water, milk, meat broth, honey, ghee, oil, and medicinal gruels — without any external or mechanical cause (such as a wound of the mouth or throat), this constitutes a powerful ariṣṭa sign. The physician should understand that the body's capacity to metabolize and assimilate (agni, the digestive fire) has become extinguished or irreversibly perverted, and that death is near.

The substances whose rejection is specifically flagged as ominous include:
1. Anna — cooked food, especially rice; the primary dietary staple.
2. Pāna — beverages in general, including plain water (udaka/jala).
3. Kṣīra — milk, considered the most wholesome and life-sustaining of all liquid foods.
4. Rasa — meat broth or the expressed juice of plant/animal materials; used therapeutically as a nourishing preparation.
5. Madhu — honey; a universal anupāna (vehicle) and preservative in Āyurvedic pharmacy.
6. Ghṛta — clarified butter (ghee); the most highly regarded of all fatty substances and a cornerstone of rasāyana (rejuvenation) therapy.
7. Taila — sesame oil; used both internally and externally.
8. Peyā — a very thin, almost liquid rice gruel prescribed for convalescent and seriously ill patients.
9. Yavāgu — a somewhat thicker rice gruel, a standard therapeutic food in fever and debility.

Conversely, when a patient suddenly craves substances entirely contrary to their usual constitution (prakṛti), season (ṛtu), or disease condition — a state called vikṛta icchā ('perverted desire') — this too is considered an ariṣṭa sign, because it signals that the doṣas (the three fundamental physiological principles: Vāta, Pitta, Kapha) have become pathologically inverted.

The chapter enunciates the principle that when the doṣas reach a state of viparītatva (complete inversion / irreversible perversion), the patient cannot survive. The physician who recognizes this combination of food and drink aversion together with other ariṣṭa signs (such as sensory loss, color changes, abnormal pulse, and peculiar odors) should refrain from initiating heroic treatment and instead provide comfort care, for further intervention is futile (nir-āśa — without hope).

Prognostic logic: In Āyurvedic physiology, hunger and thirst are governed by samāna vāyu (the sub-type of Vāta regulating digestion and assimilation) and by the state of agni (digestive fire). Complete, spontaneous aversion to food indicates that agni has been extinguished — a state equivalent to death of the metabolic principle that sustains life. Without agni, the tissues (dhātu) cannot be nourished, ojas (the essence of vitality and immunity) cannot be maintained, and death follows inevitably.

entities_mentioned.name: Anna (cooked food / rice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oryza sativa (rice) as primary referent; also cooked grain foods generally
entities_mentioned.notes: In classical Āyurveda, anna most commonly refers to cooked rice but extends to all prepared food. Its rejection is the central sign of this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Pāna (beverages / water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Water (Aqua) and beverages generally
entities_mentioned.notes: Includes plain water (udaka / jala) and all liquid preparations consumed as drink.

entities_mentioned.name: Kṣīra (milk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cow's milk (Bos taurus) primarily; also buffalo, goat milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the most nourishing of all liquid foods; its rejection signals profound depletion of ojas.

entities_mentioned.name: Rasa (meat broth / expressed juice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Meat decoction / broth, or expressed plant juices depending on context
entities_mentioned.notes: Rasa in dietary context typically refers to meat broth (māṃsa-rasa), a high-protein restorative preparation for the debilitated; the term also covers fresh plant juice.

entities_mentioned.name: Madhu (honey)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apis spp. honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used extensively as anupāna (vehicle/adjunct) in Āyurvedic preparations; considered yogavāhī (potentiating carrier). Its rejection is notable because honey is typically universally palatable.

entities_mentioned.name: Ghṛta (clarified butter / ghee)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clarified butter from cow's milk (Bos taurus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Supreme among sneha (oleaginous substances) in Āyurveda; central to snehana (oleation therapy) and rasāyana formulations. Aversion to ghee signals breakdown of the fatty-tissue (medas) nourishment pathway.

entities_mentioned.name: Taila (sesame oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum (sesame) oil
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary plant-derived fat used in Āyurvedic internal and external therapies; second only to ghee in importance among the sneha group.

entities_mentioned.name: Peyā (thin rice gruel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thin gruel of Oryza sativa
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard therapeutic food for very ill and post-treatment patients; extremely easy to digest. Rejection of peyā indicates collapse of the digestive and assimilative capacity.

entities_mentioned.name: Yavāgu (rice gruel, medium consistency)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medium-consistency gruel of Oryza sativa
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from peyā by a slightly higher grain-to-water ratio; prescribed in fever (jvara), debility, and convalescence. One of the most frequently mentioned therapeutic foods across the Charaka Samhita.

entities_mentioned.name: Agni (digestive fire)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not a plant or drug but a central physiological concept in Āyurveda; the metabolic principle governing digestion, transformation, and tissue nourishment. Its extinction (agni-nāśa) is the theoretical basis for the terminal sign described in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Doṣa (Vāta, Pitta, Kapha)
entities_mentioned.notes: The three fundamental physiological humors of Āyurveda. The chapter's principle of viparītatva (inversion of doṣas) as a sign of impending death reflects the Āyurvedic theory that health requires balanced doṣa interaction.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas (vital essence / immunity)
entities_mentioned.notes: The refined essence of all seven dhātu (body tissues); its depletion is associated with susceptibility to disease and death. Food aversion is understood to starve ojas of its nutritive substrate.

results.translator_notes:
1. The label 'Annaraka' is a compound: anna (food) + araka, likely a variant/dialect form of arocaka (distaste, aversion, anorexia) or raka meaning 'rejection.' Some editions read the chapter title as Annaroca or Annārocaka; the sense is consistent across readings — aversion to food and drink as an omen of death.

2. This chapter belongs to the Indriya-sthāna ('Sensory / Prognostic Section'), which is entirely devoted to ariṣṭa-lakṣaṇa: signs by which a physician can determine that a patient's condition is beyond cure. This section has no therapeutic prescriptions; it is purely prognostic.

3. The chapter does not describe specific plants with botanical detail but enumerates food and therapeutic substances whose rejection constitutes the clinical sign. Accordingly, the 'preparation method' field in the standard Plantacopia template is not directly applicable here — the chapter is a prognostic manual, not a pharmaceutical one.

4. Scholarly dating: The Indriya-sthāna is sometimes considered one of the oldest layers of the Charaka Samhita and may predate the main therapeutic sections. The ariṣṭa literature it contains shows parallels with the Atharva Veda and early Suśruta material.

5. The phrase 'yasya doṣā viparītatvam āpadyante, sa na jīvati' is reconstructed from the general teaching of the Indriya-sthāna chapters; the exact Sanskrit wording varies across manuscript traditions (Hemādri commentary, Cakrapāṇidatta redaction, etc.). It is presented here as a paraphrase of the chapter's principle rather than a direct quotation.

6. No OCR source text was provided for this segment; the entry is constructed from authoritative scholarly knowledge of Charaka Samhita Indriya-sthāna 12. Any reader requiring a verse-by-verse translation should consult the critical edition by P.V. Sharma or the Jamnagar translation series.
